speaker: interviewer
text: Reel 3 with Alex Reifer, reel 3. I have some questions for you before you tell me that you moved on-- before you moved on. First of all, when your family was fleeing Oswiecim, were there bombs going off? Was it real war? Was it like the blitzkrieg? Do you-- can you describe any--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was some bombs going on. And it was a lot of planes, which was bombarding small bombs-- not big bombs, small bombs. And all the time. And the time when we was running, when the plane-- the German plane, because in Poland, was no plane. In Poland, they forgot to-- to send out planes, maybe. I don't know. And, uh, the-- the planes was killing a lot of people, which was running for this, when we was running. And I-- we had a plane coming. We run to the fields, on the wilderness. We run over in the side, in the side, in Poland. It's not like here, you have a five freeway. It is a small, which a-- which a horse and wagon can go. There's no-- there's no asphalt. There's stones. And-- and in the side is wilderness. Then you run in, in the wilderness.

speaker: interviewer
text: How crowded were those roads?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very crowded. Very crowded, 24 hours a day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there lots of strafing when the planes came through? Do you know the word 'strafing' that I'm talking-- that's when the guns from the airplanes--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were shooting, yeah. They were shooting from the planes. It was a lot of problems.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you left your family, and, you know, they told you to go ahead, do you know where that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Um, it was about a couple days from Lwow. I don't know exactly the name of city, because we didn't go to cities at all. We only go in the sides to make near how to come to Lwow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you said goodbye to your family--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: If it's-- if it's too difficult, I understand. But I was wondering if you could describe for us what that was like when you said goodbye to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was crying. All of-- all of us. Because I didn't see them any longer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, was that the last time you saw your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to-- did you know where they-- did you know where they went from that point on? Or did you ever have contact?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. I-- I knew that they go-- was going back to Oswiecim. Because it's the only way they can do. I have got some-- some to start with in 1939. I've got some letters coming. They knew where I am, and they knew that I will go to Russia. They didn't know I was work in a coal mine or something, but they knew I-- I am in the Russian territory and in Lwow. Because in this particular time, Lwow before was Poland. And Russia came in, they took it, because they don't want that the German-- they make agreement with Hitler at this particular time to take this particular part of Poland. That mean they divide Poland. Germany took a part, and a part took, Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's-- that's a very famous pact. I think if you tell-- I don't want to tell it. You should tell us.

speaker: interviewee
text: See, the pact was, normally, Russia said always that the Lwow, until Lwow, or a little bit further on belongs to Ukraina and belongs to Russia. But it didn't belong to Russia. But they said that, and give us going in. And this is the pact which Hitler made with Stalin in this particular time, that he will take Lwow.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was the Stalin-Hitler--

speaker: interviewee
text: Stalin-Hitler agreement.

speaker: interviewer
text: Non-Aggression Pact, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Non-Aggression Pact. But Hitler didn't work on non-aggression.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you indicated earlier, much earlier in your testimony that you began to learn some Russian in the house because your mother--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother was speak Russian, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your Russian like by this time? When you got to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Was not-- not-- OK, I-- I spoke a little bit, but I learn Russian in the time when I was in the coal mine. I learn Russian. And I speak it in this particular time very good already.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there lots of Jews with you in the coal mine, or no? I mean, what was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Lot of Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about religion? What about your payot? What about your--

speaker: interviewee
text: No more. I don't got payot no more. No payot.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get-- when did you cut them? When did you put away--?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I-- when I walk in Sosnowiec, by the company [INAUDIBLE], [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which they sold only to-- to people which making suits in factories. I cut my payot. I couldn't be in the store with this particular payot, because I haven't got any connection.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the tefillin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tefillin, I got with me all the time, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your father react to this? That you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I came home one time, and my third brother, [INAUDIBLE], don't want to talk to me. Because I'm not religious enough to talk to him, even. We come from a very religious family, see?

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in the coal mines, did you still keep your tefillin with you, or no?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: By that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I-- when I-- when I in Lwow, I didn't got my tefillin with me, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: When your family wrote to you, once they returned to Oswiecim, what was in their letters about what was-- did they describe to you anything about--?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they-- they wrote to me certain item. They didn't describe what happened, because they couldn't-- everything was censored. And I understand. But in-- in certain wording, I understand what's going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were-- do you remember what that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember that the-- the army came a lot of time-- I mean, the police army, whatever it is. And they confiscated a lot of things in the house. This is the way that it was. And after that, when they take them to concentration camp, no more-- no more letters.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the last you heard from your family? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: The last I heard from my family was in 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, oy, oy, 1941, where I was? I was, in 1941, in Voroshilovgrad, in Russia. I came from the coal mine-- I didn't-- I start talking to you about it. Now, I decide-- when I met my friend in the hospital Leon Klappholtz, I decide to run away from the coal mine. And, but I did-- I need-- he asked me about, I need a [RUSSIAN]. I don't have. I need a paper [RUSSIAN], they calling it in Russian. But what I did-- I find out what train goes from this [NON-ENGLISH] from the fifth [NON-ENGLISH], they calling it [NON-ENGLISH], the fifth [NON-ENGLISH]. The name is Five-- [NON-ENGLISH] Five. [NON-ENGLISH] means a coal mine. Coal Mine Five. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this particular time, when the train leaves for the city of Voroshilovgrad, Voroshilovgrad was a big city. The name is Voroshilovgrad, W. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And without any papers, it's very hard in Russia to move. But I did. I took whatever I can with me. And I got in-- in the train station a couple hours before the train leaves. Nobody was there. And I got in in the train, I lay down under the-- under the things, and that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you-- lay down under what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Underneath-- underneath of the-- the seats.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not under the tracks. Not under the train, but inside--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, in the train, under the seats. It's the big seat-- down there is big seat, like not-- not individual seat. And I lay down, and I lay down there until I came to Voroshilovgrad. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was going many hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were a stowaway.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I came to Voroshilovgrad. I couldn't go out immediately, because it was a big movement. Then I wait an hour and a half, two hours. I wait, until everybody moved out from the train. And I got out from the train slowly. See, the Russian could recognize our people, because we wore [? Polish ?] hats, special hats. And, um, I took off my hat, put it in my arm. And I got in, in the station. And I got in the station. I got some money, I bought some food. And, um, I lay-- I was tired, because I was nervous in the-- in the train. I didn't slept in the train, I couldn't. And, um-- because I was underneath. Then I slept under the-- there was a lot of people in the-- in the waiting room I mean, in the-- when I came down there to Voroshilovgrad. And, um, I lay down. I slept a little bit. I was down there a day and a half, a night and a half. And uh, daytime, a woman came to me, because I got my head down. And they knew it was [? Polish ?] people have the hat, with hat, special [? Polish ?] like four-- four counts, you know, four points. And she came, and she asked me if I am Jewish. I said yes. And she said she is also Jewish. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and we was talking, and we were start talking in Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she asked me where-- what I-- what I did, what I do. I didn't know who-- who she is. And she said, you know what-- in Jewish, you know what? You come with me to my house.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said thank you. I was going with this-- with this woman to the house. I came to the house, she give me to eat food. And then she told me that she is Jewish, her husband is engineer, and the husband will be right home. And, um-- and she liked to help Jewish people, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because she is Jewish. [SOBS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And she give me to eat. And she was-- she was also working in [NON-ENGLISH]. She-- she was a [NON-ENGLISH], this woman, a [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] means you're working in [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what a-- a [NON-ENGLISH] is? But she write prescription-- if you have a prescription, you go to 'for-macy'-- Oh, 'farm-acy.'

speaker: interviewee
text: Pharmacy, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry. Sorry, I-- I'm speaking in Russian, [NON-ENGLISH]. And uh, and then she said, if you want, I give you a job to work. I said, I don't care where I work. I [INAUDIBLE] the job. She said, you can sleep here in our house, and give me a special room. And um, she talked over the phone, she talked to a friend of hers. And the friend of her is a [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what [NON-ENGLISH] means? [NON-ENGLISH] is one of the top of the pharmacists, is a [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: And the [NON-ENGLISH] was the head from the-- from [NON-ENGLISH] number one. [NON-ENGLISH]-- [NON-ENGLISH], mamma mia-- where you buy the medicines. Where you buy medicine.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the drugstore? At a pharmacy?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, pharmacy.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pharmacy. And, uh, she talked to her, and she said, you know what? Um, I will introduce you to a person. She is not Jewish, but she's a very, very nice person, and she will give you a job in the pharmacy. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I didn't even ask what should I do. I don't care, you know. And, um, she took me to the pharmacy, she introduced me to this woman. This woman name is Mazgirova Aleksandra Arkad'evna.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the woman who picked you up at the train station?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I don't-- I written down, but I don't-- I don't remember anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. But the name of the woman she took you to? She took me to, she was the-- the manager from the [NON-ENGLISH] number one. And the name was Ms. [INAUDIBLE]-- a Russian woman, Mazgirova Aleksandra Arkad'evna.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a top person in the city, also. And she took me to the basement, and she told me what to do, how to do it. She don't-- she talk in Russian, I understand, but I am not perfect in this particular time in Russian. Now, I am, but I was not. And, uh, I was filling all kind, I was filling [INAUDIBLE], in bottles for 200 gram, or 100 gram. And different type of medicine-- to fill in, how to weigh it, you know, to preparation. And I was working a long time down there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two questions for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: If I were to look at a map, where would I find I can't--

speaker: interviewee
text: Voroshilovgrad. Uh, Voroshilovgrad is not far from Ukraina. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't know where you can find Voroshilovgrad. Not far from Kiev, OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: North or south?

speaker: interviewee
text: South-- south of Kiev.

speaker: interviewer
text: East or west of?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I think it's southwest of Kiev, something like that, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this? Was this already in 1940?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. This was, uh-- this was already in-- before 1940 start-- the first year, 1940. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And, um, one time-- she-- she was a very, very nice woman, very intelligent. And then she write me one time for dinner. And, um-- she asked me, what is my life? And I told her what it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then she said, if you want to live here, I could give you a home. You can live here. And I told her that I have a friend. He is-- Leon Klappholtz, a friend of mine. He is also in the-- in the coal mine. And he is wounded, leg. If you want to help, I say, if you give me a [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH] means a piece of paper that at least I can go. Because I don't want to-- I don't want to lay down under the things. I want to go normal. She give me, and she stamp it. And I was going by the train to-- to my friend. Who He was still in the hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I talked to him. I said, you know what? I find a woman, a very nice woman. She was an older person. And, um, I think I can take you out from here. Yeah, I said, how you feel? And he said it's OK, now they got several operations. He's got-- he's got to [INAUDIBLE] several operations, but he fix it already. I said, in four weeks, he can go out. I told him, you know what? I will try to-- I will-- [DRINKS] I will try to, uh-- to come back in a-- a week, or two, or three. I will see when I can come with a [NON-ENGLISH]. I need a paper that I'm sent from this [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And, uh, I got back. Then I came in again, a couple of weeks later, about 10-- two weeks, two or two and a half weeks later, I came again. And I took him out. I have got the piece of paper for him, too, to come. And he moved in with me to this particular woman. And, uh, we was down there a certain time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was down there a couple weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I thought you meant maybe like a year or something like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SHAKES HEAD] I was-- I was down there a couple weeks. And then after that, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we-- we was trying to-- established what we can, you know. And, um-- and I think-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- what I was doing is this way, because this Leon Klappholtz, his father was-- has got a butcher store. And he was helping. In Poland, he was helping his father.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to the-- to the Ms. Mazgirova Aleksandra Arkad'evna, [INAUDIBLE] the woman-- I said, is it possible we find for him a job? She said, what can he do? I said, he can do some cutting meat, something like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: She said yes, she has a friend which has-- which has-- he is the manager, the director from a big restaurant. She will talk to him. And, um, she talked to him. And I was going to my friend, and I bring him over. And he-- he was-- he was-- I introduced him. I was going with the woman and with my friend to this restaurant. And we introduced, and the-- and the general manager from the restaurant was from Poland. I mean, he was from Poland, maybe many years ago, but-- his name was Stanislav Runicki. I never forget. He was a very nice person. His name was Stanislav Runicki. And he hired this friend of mine. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He hired him. And he will start working from 8:00 until 4:30, 5 o'clock, 6 o'clock. He was working in the kitchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was wondering if we could sort of just change course a little bit here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't mean to cut off your testimony.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I think we need-- I need to keep us focused.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I think what's important about your testimony is how you were draft-- how you wound up in the Russian army and what happened to you subsequently. If there is something that you feel that is of great importance to talk about before you were in the army, this is the time to do it. But I think that your testimony is so unusual that we should-- unless you feel you-- I don't want to insult you in any way. So do you want to talk-- is there something that you feel, because you were uprooted, you need to talk about? Or would you-- can we move ahead to when you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: We can move ahead, but if you want, I can tell you what I did, also, I mean--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, I do-- I only-- I really need to know just about you, rather than focus on your friend Leon, for instance. We need to really only focus and keep our vision strictly with you, OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I told you before that I am very handy with the hand. And, um, I was also try to get into restaurant business. I mean, not me, there aren't any can own, but-- and, um, I was introduced to make parties. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, uh, I made parties. All carving, ice carving, centerpiece carving, all kind of stuff. And I did it, and I was successful. And I did it, and, um-- they open up some new restaurants, which I made it. I organize it. And I was very famous down there.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the Soviet Union?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Soviet Union.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was what, when? 19-- what years?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- this was in 1940, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] before the war-- the war started 22nd of June, 1941. Started war between Germany and Russia. And this was in 1940. And I lived by this woman, Mazgirova [INAUDIBLE] Aleksandra Arkad'evna, in with my friend. But, um, before the war start, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they, um, drafted us-- sorry. They drafted us, preparation for the army. Not to the army preparation, but army-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I got registered, and that's all that it was. But when the war start already, in 1941, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the-- see, I was drafted before I was already an Amer-- a Russian citizen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that because you had papers? I'm a little confused. You had the Russia-- you had Soviet papers, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did that allow them to draft you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because I'm Soviet-- Soviet citizen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait, you just said you were drafted before you were a Soviet citizen.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, when you come to the Russian-- the Russian country, and, um-- and you have papers, and done the interview, you-- and you became a Russian citizen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you became a citizen in--

speaker: interviewee
text: I became-- I became a citizen when I came-- when I came to Voroshilovgrad.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that enabled them, allowed them to draft you into the army.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. I received papers to-- the drafting papers. And I-- I go down there, and they draft me to the army. I was drafted in 1941, and they send me to our group, to a city, the name is Frunze. Frunze is a city with very warm climate in Russia. And, um, in this city, they register me in the-- with knowledge which I have, that I speak German and different languages. Then they put me in reconnaissance. Reconnaissance means not FBI, but, uh-- reconnaissance is like, um--

speaker: interviewee
text: In English, is reconnaissance? In English, is reconnaissance. OK, they put me in reconnaissance.

speaker: interviewee
text: Down there, it is different. It is [NON-ENGLISH]. In Russia, it is [NON-ENGLISH], they calling it. And, uh, I study that. And I, uh, was attached to a certain group, and they send me to the 385th Division. Was organizing division, these people in this particular time. The 385th Division, and they put me in a [NON-ENGLISH]. A [NON-ENGLISH] is a regiment, regiment. The 1266 regiment-- 1266. Each division has three regiments. It was 1266, 1268, and 1270. And they put me in the 1266 regiment, and I was like a soldier from the reconnaissance group. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After that, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the-- when we finished studying everything, they put us to the front. They give us uniforms, I am very good in shooting before that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you learn?

speaker: interviewee
text: I learn-- I learn from-- from when I was a kid, I learn shooting.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was in-- in school about 11-- 10, 11 years old, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we was going for sport, we was going to shooting [INAUDIBLE]. And I was shooting. I was good, very good. I'm good today, too. Not now-- I don't kill anybody. I killed a lot of people, but I don't kill anybody today. I mean, I killed a lot of Germans. A lot of them. OK, now, what it was-- we was going, we were going to school. And I-- I became-- I was going to school as a sergeant, sergeant school, to be a private soldier. And then they send us to the front-- the whole division, the 385th Division, they send us to the front. And, um--

speaker: interviewer
text: But the front was very large.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very far.

speaker: interviewer
text: And large. Where-- where in the front were you sent?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, I became-- I came to the front, which it was-- about-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the [INAUDIBLE] section, not far from Lwow, on the border from Lwow. But, um, we was not on the front immediately to start with. We was behind the front to teach more, to learn more about the front. They don't throw you out right away in the front. And I was on the-- I was-- on the front already. When I finished school, and I was already [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH]. This was in 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you repeat that, because these are Russian--

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] is [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] is in charge of-- normally of 45 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that--

speaker: interviewee
text: He had-- you have a V shape, two V shapes. This is [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: So like a sergeant, or a corporal?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH], higher sergeant, yeah. Not corporal-- corporal is below that. and [NON-ENGLISH] is in charge of corporal, of sergeant, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me just take you back. I want to ask you a couple of very specific Jewish questions.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the treatment like of Jews in the Soviet Union between 1939 and 1941? Do you remember how-- I guess you can only speak for yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: See, the-- not for myself, but in-- in regular life, from the Russian people, you have about 60% anti-Semitism. The 40% is-- is the people which are more intelligent, which they're going to the high school, to universities. There, also, you have anti-Semitism, but not so much of it. The plain people, because when they put it in his head, 'He's a-- a dirty Jew,' and this he says, he doesn't think that the person which he calls 'dirty Jew' is also a human being. He doesn't think that at all. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He thinks-- that what he told him that's all what it is-- a dirty Jew. But in the army, we have got in the Russian army, we have got a lot of anti-Semitism, too. I got-- I got, when I was [NON-ENGLISH], I got-- I got, in my groups, several anti-Semitism. One guy was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we're going to talk about him on the next reel, OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm, yeah.
